Diffusion  and  degradation  are  two  main  release  mechanisms  of
biodegradable microspheres [20,21]. The vitro release of microspheres
showed  a  typical  three-phase  release  pattern  (Fig.  6)  [22].  Namely,
initial burst release phase (phase I, 0–4 d), lag phase (phase II, 5–20 d),
and  later  constant  rate release  phase (phase  III,  21–63  d).  The initial
burst release can be understood as the rapid release of LNG attached to
the surface of the microspheres [23]. Although the microspheres were
washed three times using distilled water in the process of preparation,
the surface of microspheres would still be attached with drugs, which
may lead to the burst release. Later, LNG on the outside of microspheres
was released completely. As the water molecules get into the interior of
